data.title: Japan economic update: Retail sales surge 1.7% on stimulus while industrial output stumbles
data.description:
Japan’s economy presented a stark contrast in March 2026, as resilient consumer spending fueled by government stimulus offset a unexpected contraction in industrial activity. While retail sales comfortably beat market expectations.

Retail sales in Japan rose by 1.7% year-over-year in March 2026, rebounding from a revised 0.1% decline in February and exceeding market predictions of 0.8%. This increase reflects the government's stimulus efforts to boost spending. 

On a monthly basis, retail trade grew 1.3%, rebounding from a 2.0% fall in February.

Meanwhile, industrial production fell by 0.5% month-on-month, below the 1.1% expected, marking a second consecutive decline due to external challenges.

On an annual basis, output increased 2.3%, picking up from a 0.4% rise in February and marking the fastest pace since June 2025.

The Nikkei 225 Index dropped 1% to around 59,300, while the broader Topix Index fell 1.6% to 3,710 in post-holiday trading on Thursday. The Japanese yen weakened past the critical 160 per dollar threshold on Thursday, reaching its lowest level since July 2024.

data.title: Standard Chartered tops forecasts, downplays Gulf conflict risks
data.description:
UK's Standard Chartered reported a strong Q1 performance with pretax profit of about $2.45 billion, up roughly 17% Y/Y and ahead of forecasts of $2.14B. Its EPS was 0.742, up 31% Y/Y

The beat was driven by strong net new money inflows into its wealth management business, which has become one of the bank’s key growth engines across Asia, the Middle East, and Africa.

Net interest income rose 1% to $2.9B, while non-interest income climbed 16% to $3B, driven by strong growth in Wealth Solutions and Global Banking.

Wealth Solutions posted a record quarter, with income up 32% on robust investment products and bancassurance, while Global Banking rose 19% on higher deal activity and capital markets volumes.

The company kept its 2026 outlook unchanged: operating income is expected to grow at the lower end of the 5%–7% range Y/Y; cost expected to be flat, while its statutory RoTE greater than 12%.

"Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and global economic uncertainty, our advantaged market presence and disciplined risk management give us confidence in our ability to perform. We continue to support our clients as they manage their businesses and wealth across borders, and we look forward to setting out our next phase of growth at our Investor Event next month." said Bill Winters CEO.

data.title: Société Générale Société anonyme Non-GAAP EPS of €2.05, revenue of €7.11B
data.description:
• Société Générale Société anonyme press release (SCGLY): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €2.05.
  • Revenue of €7.11B (+0.4% Y/Y).
  • Cost-to-income ratio of 60.9%2 in Q1 26 vs. 65.0% in Q1 25.
  • Group net income of €1,696 million in Q1 26, +5.5% vs. Q1 25.
  • Profitability (ROTE) of 11.7%.
  • Completion on 18 March 2026 of the 2025 ordinary share buy-back programme of €1,462 million, launched on 9 February 2026.
  • CET1 ratio of 13.5% at end of Q1 26.
  • Liquidity Coverage Ratio of 149% at end of Q1 26.

data.title: Red Rock outlines $385M Durango North expansion opening in summer 2027 while holding 2026 capex at $375M-$425M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Red Rock Resorts (RRR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “The first quarter represented another strong quarter for the company across all key measures,” and “our Las Vegas operations delivered the highest first quarter net revenue and the second highest first quarter adjusted EBITDA in our history while maintaining near record adjusted EBITDA margin,” according to (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Stephen Cootey).
  • On Durango, (CFO Cootey) said, “we remain very pleased with Durango's performance and the successful revenue backfill at our core properties,” adding that “Durango continues to expand in the Las Vegas Locals market and drive incremental play from our existing customers.”
  • On the next major growth project at Durango, (CFO Cootey) said, “the Durango North expansion… is scheduled to open in the summer of 2027 with a total cost estimated at approximately $385 million,” and will add “more than 275,000 square feet… including nearly 400 additional slot machines” plus “a 36-lane bowling facility, luxury movie theaters,” and “our partnership with Moonshine Flats.”
  • “On a consolidated basis, our first quarter net revenue… was $507.3 million,” and “we converted 50.3% of our adjusted EBITDA into operating free cash flow, generating $107 million or $1.03 per share,” (CFO Cootey) said.

Outlook

  • (CFO Cootey) said, “as we look ahead into the second quarter, we are seeing stable trends in our core slot and table business… consistent with a return to more typical seasonal patterns,” while also noting “continued near-term disruption from our ongoing construction at and around Durango Sunset Station and Green Valley Ranch.”
  • (CFO Cootey) reiterated capital spending expectations: “for the full year 2026, we expect to spend between $375 million and $425 million,” including “$275 million to $300 million in investment capital” and “$100 million to $125 million in maintenance capital.”
  • On leverage tolerance as new projects are considered, (CFO Cootey) said, “while we'd love to keep maintaining leverage on and around 4 for the right opportunities that we would spike leverage up,” and “once you start topping five, that's really where you start… getting a little concerned.”

Financial results

  • Consolidated net revenue of $507.3 million vs. $505,653,000.0 (analysts estimate).
  • (CFO Cootey) reported Las Vegas operations “first quarter net revenue was $499.5 million” and “adjusted EBITDA was $232.4 million,” while consolidated “adjusted EBITDA… was $212.6 million” and “adjusted EBITDA margin was 41.9% for the quarter.”
  • On margins and costs, (CFO Cootey) said “the majority of the EBITDA margin degradation can be contributed to the Green Valley room… disruption,” while also citing “elevated utilities costs… as well as loss and some loss of damages,” and added, “payroll is probably up a little under 3%… COGS… flat to down.”
  • On shareholder returns and capital allocation, (CFO Cootey) said the company paid a “special dividend of $1 per Class A common share,” a “quarterly dividend of $0.26,” and repurchased “approximately 635,000… shares at an average price of $60.32,” and “when combining the dividends and the share repurchases… we returned approximately $170.5 million to shareholders.”

Q&A

  • Zachary Silverberg, Wells Fargo: asked to “quantify” impacts from “higher gas prices” and “air travel”; (CFO Cootey) responded, “we've seen no impact from higher gas prices,” and on air travel disruption said “the TSA impact has been de minimis.”
  • Zachary Silverberg, Wells Fargo: asked on seasonality and one-timers; (CFO Cootey) said “Q1 is definitely our peak quarter… Q1 to Q2, generally were down 8% to 9%,” and added “another $9 million of disruption… in Q2” plus “another $2 million to $3 million of disruption starting next quarter” at Durango.
  • Barry Jonas, Truist: asked about Durango North disruption through July 2027; (CFO Cootey) said “this is early days in a $385 million construction project” and “the $2 million to $3 million estimate for disruption sticks pretty much through the summer.”
  • John DeCree, CBRE: asked about margin decline drivers and hotel demand; (CFO Cootey) said the Green Valley disruption was “probably almost half” the margin degradation, while (President Scott Kreeger) said Green Valley’s renovated product is driving “increased ADR growth,” and “we're optimistic about the summer, but it's really early in the booking window.”
  • Chad Beynon, Macquarie: asked “maximum leverage”; (CFO Cootey) said “we kind of articulate right now we're about 4.07x,” and “we would spike leverage up,” but flagged concern “once you start topping five.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on “disruption,” margin pressure, and consumer sensitivity (e.g., “Could you quantify those 2 buckets” and “unpack… OpEx inflation?”).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing resilience and visibility, including “we remain highly confident,” “we like what we're seeing in April,” and a clear disruption framework (e.g., “That is correct, Dan”).
  • Versus last quarter, management used more quantified disruption language (e.g., “another $9 million… in Q2” and “$2 million to $3 million”) compared with Q4’s repeated “gut feel” and “not ready to give guidance on that one just yet.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 emphasized headwinds “later in the quarter including higher gas prices, air travel-related disruption and temporary construction impacts,” while Q4 emphasized “new fourth quarter records” and “ninth consecutive record quarter.”
  • Q1 added specificity on construction impacts, including “another $9 million of disruption… in Q2” and Durango disruption “$2 million to $3 million… through the summer,” whereas in Q4 management said Durango and Sunset impacts were “more of a gut feel.”
  • Balance sheet positioning shifted modestly, with Q1 reporting “net debt-to-EBITDA ratio was 4.07x” vs. Q4’s “3.87x,” alongside continued emphasis on flexibility and willingness to “spike leverage up” for projects.

Risks and concerns

  • (CFO Cootey) cited “continued near-term disruption” at “Durango Sunset Station and Green Valley Ranch,” and quantified that “we're expecting another $9 million of disruption to occur in Q2” at Green Valley, plus Durango disruption “$2 million to $3 million.”
  • On uncertainty around consumer/traffic conditions, (VP & Vice Chairman Lorenzo Fertitta) said March was “affected… by gas, by the war, the uncertainty as well as just the TSA situation,” while (CFO Cootey) added April was “tracking to be 1 of the best Aprils on record.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as historically strong on Las Vegas net revenue and gaming profitability while acknowledging measurable, near-term construction disruption (particularly at Green Valley Ranch and Durango) and pointing investors to the next major catalysts: the $385 million Durango North expansion targeted for summer 2027, a fully financed North Fork project slated for early Q4 2026, and a 2026 capital plan of $375 million to $425 million alongside continued dividends and share repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apollo, Blackstone and KKR in race for Shell’s LNG Canada stake: Reuters
data.description:
Apollo Global Management (APO), Blackstone (BX) and ​KKR (KKR) are battling it out to acquire a significant stake in the mammoth LNG Canada project from energy major ‌Shell (SHEL), three people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

The trio, among the world's largest asset management firms, are the remaining bidders in the auction process, run by Shell, which also garnered interest from other large money managers and infrastructure investors.

Any deal is expected to be valued well north of $10B and ​could reach as high as $15B, according to some of the people.

The sale will allow Shell, which on Monday announced a $16.4B deal ⁠to buy Canadian natural gas producer ARC Resources (AETUF), an opportunity to sell a big portion of its 40% stake in LNG Canada and ​attract new capital to the export project ahead of the facility's possible expansion.

LNG Canada, which started production in June, is the first major liquefied ​natural gas facility in North America with direct access to the Pacific, allowing it to ship straight to buyers in Asia, the largest market for the super-cooled fuel.

Shell (SHEL) plans to sell exposure in the first and second phases of the project to one bidder, rather than splitting the two parts, which ​was a possibility when Reuters first reported the sale process in January, some of the sources added.

data.title: Samsung Electronics Q1 profit jumps eightfold on AI chip demand
data.description:
• Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) reported an over eightfold jump in Q1 operating profit, beating analyst estimates and setting a new quarterly record, driven by solid growth in its semiconductor business.
  • Its operating profit rose to 57.2T Korean won, ahead of the 55.28T won forecast by LSEG SmartEstimate, while revenue came in at 133.9T won versus expectations of 132.69T won. Profit surged more than 750% from a year earlier, with revenue climbing about 70%, both marking record highs for the company.
  • Division-wise, the Device Solutions Division posted a Q/Q sales increase of 86%, with the Memory Business setting an all-time high for quarterly revenue and operating profit. While, the Device eXperience Division posted a QoQ sales increase of 19%, supported by the launch of new flagship smartphones. 
  • "In H2 2026, the Company expects server memory demand to remain strong as hyperscalers accommodate enterprises’ increasing adoption of AI and LLM services. Additionally, agentic AI is expected to accelerate growth in demand." the company said.

data.title: ArcelorMittal reports mixed Q1 results; reaffirms FY26 capex outlook
data.description:
• ArcelorMittal S.A. press release (MT): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.76 beats by $0.05.
  • Revenue of $15.46B (+4.5% Y/Y) misses by $600M.
  • The Group's results continue to demonstrate resilience, with 1Q 2026 EBITDA per tonne of $131/t increasing by $15/t year-on-year.
  • A typical seasonal working capital investment of $1.5bn during the quarter led to a free cash outflow of $1.3bn and an increase in net debt to $9.3B.
  • Liquidity remains at a robust $9.9B and the company's positive FCF outlook for 2026 and beyond remains unchanged.
  • 2026 capex guidance remains unchanged at $4.5bn-$5.0bn and includes $1.7-$2.0bn of strategic capex on high return projects.

data.title: ING Group reports Q1 results; reaffirms outlook for 2026 and 2027
data.description:
• ING Group press release (ING): Q1 four-quarter rolling average return on tangible equity of 13.9%; CET1 ratio of 13.0%.
  • "We announce a share buyback programme of €1.0 billion as we continue to return capital in excess of our ~13% target to our shareholders."
  • Total income of €5.82B (+3.2% Y/Y) misses by €40M.
  • Outlook for 2026 and 2027: We confirm our outlook for 2026 and 2027. Based on current assumptions and scenarios, we expect a total income of around €24 billion in 2026. This outlook is supported by volume growth and an anticipated 5-10% increase in fee income. Total operating expenses (excluding incidental cost items) are projected to be in the range of €12.6-€12.8 billion in 2026. For the full year 2026, we expect an effective tax rate of 29-31%. We maintain our CET1 capital ratio guidance at ~13%. The return on tangible equity (ROTE) is expected to exceed 14% for full-year 2026. For 2027, we expect total income to exceed €25 billion, including more than €5 billion in fee income. And we anticipate operating expenses (excluding incidental cost items) of around €13 billion, an effective tax rate of 29-31% and an ROTE above 15%. We maintain our CET1 capital ratio guidance at ~13%.

data.title: Pulmonx reiterates $90M-$92M 2026 revenue guidance amid cost cuts and expects return to growth in 2H 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pulmonx (LUNG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Pulmonx delivered total worldwide revenue of $20.6 million in the first quarter of 2026" and "we remain confident in our ability to achieve our previously communicated revenue guidance of $90 million to $92 million for the full year 2026 with a return to global growth in the back half of this year" (CEO, President & Director Glendon French).
  • "We have filled with top talent all our sales leadership positions and substantially all our U.S. field sales roles" and "sales turnover has stabilized over the last 6 months" as the company narrows sales priorities under its "near-to-far approach" (CEO French).
  • "Our CONVERT II pivotal trial is progressing well" and "we are highly confident in our ability to complete enrollment of this trial in 2027" to "expand our total addressable market by approximately 20% globally" (CEO French).
  • "We executed a broad cost reduction initiative in the first quarter" and "we remain on track to deliver meaningful operating leverage and lower cash burn" (CEO French).
  • "Total worldwide revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $20.6 million" and "the decline in revenue was fully attributable to the absence of sales to our distributor in China" while "excluding China" international markets "grew 22%" year-over-year (CFO & COO Derrick Sung).
  • "We closed on a $60 million credit facility with a 5-year interest-only structure" that "extend[s] the maturity of our existing debt out to 2031" and provides "access to an additional $20 million in undrawn capital subject to certain revenue milestones" (CFO Sung).

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating our full year 2026 revenue guidance of $90 million to $92 million" (CFO Sung).
  • "Our guidance contemplates sequential quarterly improvement in our year-over-year revenue trend with a return to year-over-year growth in both our U.S. and international businesses in the back half of the year" (CFO Sung).
  • "In the U.S., we expect our recently filled sales positions and our refocused commercial strategy to gradually drive improving sales productivity" and internationally "growth through the first half of 2026 will continue to be negatively impacted by the lack of sales to our distributor in China" with resumption in the second half (CFO Sung).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management again used the same core framing that "2026" is "a year of execution and transition" and reiterated the same full-year revenue range and second-half return-to-growth posture (CEO French; CFO Sung).

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue of $20.6M vs. $20.4314M estimate (CFO Sung; analystsEstimates).
  • Q1 loss per share of (-$0.33) vs. (-$0.33) estimate (CFO Sung; analystsEstimates).
  • "Gross margin for the first quarter of 2026 was 78%" and "we continue to expect gross margin to be approximately 75% for the full year of 2026" due to distributor-mix dynamics (CFO Sung).
  • "Operating expenses in the first quarter included approximately $1.4 million of onetime costs related to the restructuring initiative" and full-year operating expenses are expected to be "$113 million to $115 million" (CFO Sung).
  • "We ended March 31, 2026, with $61.6 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities" and "we expect to burn roughly $23 million of cash for the full year 2026" (CFO Sung).

Q&A

  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: asked for "specific strategies" to "see sales growth accelerate" and what’s "dialed into the guidance"; CEO French responded, "we have been focused on narrowing the items that we're asking our U.S. sales force to do" and said the team is "right where we expected to be at this point."
  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: pressed on quarterly sequencing and confidence in a 2H step-up; CFO Sung said the company expects "sequential" improvement, cited "we've bottomed in Q4," and stated it expects to exit 2026 with "double-digit growth, both U.S. and international," while noting China is "less than 5%" of sales.
  • John Young, Canaccord: asked about "15" new U.S. treating centers and ongoing pace plus "metrics"; CEO French said, "It's difficult to say whether that's anywhere close to the new normal" and added "we're going to stand with the 10 per quarter expectation" while pointing to StratX as a leading indicator.
  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street: asked for longer-term growth aspirations; CEO French said, "I certainly expect us to be a double-digit grower," and CFO Sung added 2026 guidance "contemplates double-digit growth as we exit the year."
  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street: asked if China renewal is relied on and risks; CFO Sung said renewal is "an administrative process" and guidance "doesn't contemplate a significant contribution from China" with resumption "very gradual."
  • Joseph Downing, Piper Sandler: asked how the plan depends on low-performing accounts; CEO French said focus is biased to "the accounts that are performing best" and noted standards for new accounts have "raised the bar."
  • Joseph Downing, Piper Sandler: asked about LungTraX Detect usage and ROI thresholds; CEO French said, "I wouldn't call it a deemphasis at all" and described a "more focused approach" because it "fits into a specific subset" and "takes longer to get set up."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on sequencing and sustainability, including questions about whether revenue should return "sooner" and how the company can "have the step-up needed" (Frederick Wise, Stifel; prior-quarter tone).
  • Management was slightly positive and execution-focused, repeatedly emphasizing control and progress, including "we remain confident" and "we are right where we expected to be at this point" (CEO French; CFO Sung).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management kept a similar message but used more progress language, including "increasingly encouraged" and "early progress," while analysts continued to probe U.S. ramp timing and China optics (CEO French; CFO Sung).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Management maintained the same three priorities (U.S. reacceleration, TAM expansion via AeriSeal, and cost alignment) and again tied U.S. improvement to rep ramp time and narrowed initiatives (CEO French; Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).
  • The China dynamic stayed central, with Q1 explicitly attributing the international decline to "absence of sales" to the China distributor and reiterating renewal expected in 2H 2026, consistent with the prior call (CFO Sung).
  • Q&A broadened from Q4’s emphasis on diagnosing 2025 salesforce disruption toward Q1’s focus on cadence mechanics (sequential improvement, exit-rate expectations) and the durability of growth claims (CFO Sung; CEO French).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Internal operational and executional challenges" and the need to "reaccelerat[e] U.S. sales growth" remained the core risk area, with management emphasizing continued rep onboarding and execution consistency (CEO French).
  • International variability tied to regulatory timing: the company said it is "awaiting the renewal of our Chinese registration certificate" and expects it "in the second half of 2026" (CFO Sung).
  • Profitability and liquidity focus remained explicit, including restructuring charges, cash burn targets, and reliance on a new credit facility with milestone-based access to incremental capital (CFO Sung).

Final Takeaway

Pulmonx management reiterated its $90M-$92M 2026 revenue guidance and described Q1 as early evidence of improving execution, citing a largely rebuilt U.S. commercial organization, a more focused selling model, and cost actions intended to reduce full-year cash burn to roughly $23M. Leadership tied the near-term international headwind to the ongoing pause in China distributor sales pending a registration renewal expected in 2H 2026, while continuing to position AeriSeal’s CONVERT II trial—targeted for enrollment completion in 2027—as a pathway to expand the global addressable market by about 20%.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: KKR explores potential $10B deal for ex-Unilever spread business - report
data.description:
KKR & Co. (KKR) is exploring a potential sale of its spreads business, formerly owned by Unilever (UL), in a deal that could value the unit at around $10B, according to a report by the Financial Times.

The business, now known as Flora Food Group, was carved out of Unilever’s (UL) portfolio and includes well-known margarine and spreads brands such as Flora and Country Crock.

The U.S. private equity firm is working with advisers on a potential sale of Flora and could draw interest from other buyout firms, the report added.

KKR (KKR) acquired the spreads unit from Unilever in 2017 for about €6.8B at that time, as the consumer goods giant sought to streamline its portfolio and focus on faster-growing categories.

The private equity firm previously attempted a sale at that valuation, but the process was shelved in April 2024 after negotiations with the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund, ADQ, collapsed due to disagreements over pricing.
